story: You wake in the cramped study of a long-departed coastal surveyor. The surveyor left behind a color legend, a pinned map of local landmarks, and a ledger of notable dates — somewhere in these papers is the six-letter code that will unlatch the sealed door. The room smells of salt and ink; every scrap appears placed to help decode that final lock.

inspectionData.information: A damp, folded reference sheet lists three color-letter pairs written in a hurried hand: Red = R, Amber = A, Violet = V. The header reads 'Pin legend — quick ref.'

A corkboard map of the bay has three prominent pins: a red pin at the lighthouse, an amber pin at the harbour pier, and a violet pin at the old oak on the headland. Each pin is labeled but contains no letters — only the colored placement.
A torn ledger lists three events with exact dates: 05 May 2010 — Arrival; 14 Feb 2004 — Founding; 24 Dec 1998 — Discovery. Each line is underlined, as if these days are significant for code assembly.
A small sticky note explains the conversion rule: 'Turn day numbers into letters: 1=A, 2=B ... 26=Z (wrap if >26).' The note seems placed next to the ledger as an instruction for those dates.
